title: As global warming threatens corals, scientists search for reefs that can take the heat
gist: Woods Hole scientist Anne Cohen's Super Reefs project uses computer modeling, lab heat-tolerance testing, AI, and a robotic surface vehicle (Yellowfin) to find and protect heat-resilient 'super reefs' in the Marshall Islands and beyond, aiming to build a multi-nation 'super reef blue corridor' for coral restoration.

insights.claim: Since 2023, record marine heat waves drove the most severe global coral bleaching event ever recorded, impacting more than 80% of the world's reefs in at least 83 countries and territories, with many not recovering.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Quantifies the systemic threat to reef-dependent fisheries and coastal economies; an escalating climate-impact baseline.

insights.claim: The world has already lost more than half its coral reefs, and some scientists warn more than 90% of tropical reefs could disappear within 25 years without significant intervention.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Frames the urgency and addressable scope for reef-restoration technology and conservation funding.

insights.claim: The Yellowfin robotic surface vehicle can capture up to 20,000 images while surveying 40 miles of reef in a single day — far more than a team of 100 divers could cover — enabling year-over-year comparison to identify heat-resistant coral colonies.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Robotic + AI monitoring dramatically scales reef survey throughput, a force-multiplier for conservation-tech.
insights.quote: Yellowfin can now capture up to 20,000 images while surveying 40 miles of reef in a single day.

insights.claim: Cohen's lab is training AI models to automatically analyze Yellowfin's reef images and build 3D reef models, replacing hours of manual image review and capturing colony-position effects on heat exposure.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: AI-automated analysis is a concrete applied use case in environmental science scaling.
insights.locator: Recently, her team at the Cohen Lab

insights.claim: The Super Reefs methodology combines a decade-scale temperature/current model, in-lab heat-stress ranking of coral samples, and current-based larval-dispersal modeling to identify and protect heat-tolerant reefs that can reseed others.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: A repeatable scientific framework for prioritizing conservation spend where it has the greatest reseeding impact.

insights.claim: Using scientifically proven heat-resilient corals as a supply chain for restoration is a very new approach that grew out of the Super Reefs project, contrasting with traditional restoration that replants bleaching-vulnerable corals with low long-term success.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Shifts restoration strategy toward heat-selected stock; a methodological edge for effective reef rebuilding.
insights.quote: The idea of using them as a supply chain for restoration projects is something that really grew out of the Super Reefs project

insights.claim: Cohen proposes a first 'super reef blue corridor' — a current-connected network of protected heat-tolerant reefs across the Marshall Islands, Kiribati, and Tuvalu — estimated to require about $10 million plus political and community buy-in.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: A scalable, fundable conservation model with a concrete budget that could serve as a global proof of concept.
insights.quote: We want to create the first 'super reef blue corridor' across millions of square kilometers of ocean, connecting the Marshall Islands, Kiribati, and Tuvalu

insights.claim: A 2021 World Bank analysis shows 40% of existing buildings in Majuro, capital of the Marshall Islands, are endangered by rising sea levels, with much of the population only a few feet above sea level and reliant on reef-dependent fishing.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Quantifies existential climate exposure of low-lying Pacific nations, relevant to adaptation finance and migration risk.

insights.claim: Forecasters warn El Niño conditions have re-formed in the tropical Pacific and are expected to strengthen by fall 2026, raising the risk of another reef-damaging marine heat wave in the Marshall Islands.
insights.speaker: Inside Climate News
insights.so_what: Near-term El Niño escalation signals elevated bleaching and fisheries risk in the coming months.
